question:
Let  $n$  be a positive integer. Starting with the sequence
$1, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{3}, \dots, \frac{1}{n}$,
form a new sequence of  $n-1$  entries
$\frac{3}{4}, \frac{5}{12}, \dots, \frac{2n-1}{2n(n-1)}$
by taking the averages of
two consecutive entries in the first sequence. Repeat the
averaging of neighbors on the second sequence to obtain a third
sequence of  $n-2$  entries, and continue until the final sequence produced
consists of a single number  $x_n$.  Show that  $x_n < 2/n$.
solution:
It is easy to see by induction that the $j$-th entry of the $k$-th
sequence (where the original sequence is $k=1$) is
$\sum_{i=1}^k \binom{k-1}{i-1}/(2^{k-1} (i+j-1))$, and so
$x_n = \frac{1}{2^{n-1}} \sum_{i=1}^n \binom{n-1}{i-1}/i$.
Now $\binom{n-1}{i-1}/i = \binom{n}{i}/n$; hence
\[
x_n = \frac{1}{n2^{n-1}} \sum_{i=1}^n \binom{n}{i}
= \frac{2^n-1}{n 2^{n-1}} < 2/n,
\]
as desired.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $\text{totalcount}$ be a positive integer. Starting with the sequence
$1, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{3}, \dots, \frac{1}{\text{totalcount}}$,
form a new sequence of $\text{totalcount}-1$ entries
$\frac{3}{4}, \frac{5}{12}, \dots, \frac{2\text{totalcount}-1}{2\text{totalcount}(\text{totalcount}-1)}$
by taking the averages of
two consecutive entries in the first sequence. Repeat the
averaging of neighbors on the second sequence to obtain a third
sequence of $\text{totalcount}-2$ entries, and continue until the final sequence produced
consists of a single number $\text{finalvalue}$.  Show that $\text{finalvalue} < 2/\text{totalcount}$.
descriptive_long.solution:
It is easy to see by induction that the $\text{sequenceindex}$-th entry of the $\text{stageindex}$-th
sequence (where the original sequence is $\text{stageindex}=1$) is
$\sum_{\text{termindex}=1}^{\text{stageindex}} \binom{\text{stageindex}-1}{\text{termindex}-1}/(2^{\text{stageindex}-1} (\text{termindex}+\text{sequenceindex}-1))$, and so
$\text{finalvalue} = \frac{1}{2^{\text{totalcount}-1}} \sum_{\text{termindex}=1}^{\text{totalcount}} \binom{\text{totalcount}-1}{\text{termindex}-1}/\text{termindex}$.  
Now $\binom{\text{totalcount}-1}{\text{termindex}-1}/\text{termindex} = \binom{\text{totalcount}}{\text{termindex}}/\text{totalcount}$; hence
\[
\text{finalvalue} = \frac{1}{\text{totalcount}2^{\text{totalcount}-1}} \sum_{\text{termindex}=1}^{\text{totalcount}} \binom{\text{totalcount}}{\text{termindex}}
= \frac{2^{\text{totalcount}}-1}{\text{totalcount}\, 2^{\text{totalcount}-1}} < 2/\text{totalcount},
\]
as desired.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let  $dragonfly$  be a positive integer. Starting with the sequence
$1, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{3}, \dots, \frac{1}{dragonfly}$,
form a new sequence of  $dragonfly-1$  entries
$\frac{3}{4}, \frac{5}{12}, \dots, \frac{2dragonfly-1}{2dragonfly(dragonfly-1)}$
by taking the averages of
two consecutive entries in the first sequence. Repeat the
averaging of neighbors on the second sequence to obtain a third
sequence of  $dragonfly-2$  entries, and continue until the final sequence produced
consists of a single number  $butterscotch$.  Show that  $butterscotch < 2/dragonfly$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
It is easy to see by induction that the $waterfall$-th entry of the $pineapple$-th
sequence (where the original sequence is $pineapple=1$) is
$\sum_{kingfish=1}^{pineapple} \binom{pineapple-1}{kingfish-1}/(2^{pineapple-1} (kingfish+waterfall-1))$, and so
$butterscotch = \frac{1}{2^{dragonfly-1}} \sum_{kingfish=1}^{dragonfly} \binom{dragonfly-1}{kingfish-1}/kingfish$.
Now $\binom{dragonfly-1}{kingfish-1}/kingfish = \binom{dragonfly}{kingfish}/dragonfly$; hence
\[
butterscotch = \frac{1}{dragonfly2^{dragonfly-1}} \sum_{kingfish=1}^{dragonfly} \binom{dragonfly}{kingfish}
= \frac{2^{dragonfly}-1}{dragonfly 2^{dragonfly-1}} < 2/dragonfly,
\]
as desired.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let  $negativebound$  be a positive integer. Starting with the sequence
$1, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{3}, \dots, \frac{1}{negativebound}$,
form a new sequence of  $negativebound-1$  entries
$\frac{3}{4}, \frac{5}{12}, \dots, \frac{2negativebound-1}{2negativebound(negativebound-1)}$
by taking the averages of
two consecutive entries in the first sequence. Repeat the
averaging of neighbors on the second sequence to obtain a third
sequence of  $negativebound-2$  entries, and continue until the final sequence produced
consists of a single number  $startnumber$.  Show that  $startnumber < 2/negativebound$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
It is easy to see by induction that the $fixedcounter$-th entry of the $settledlevel$-th
sequence (where the original sequence is $settledlevel=1$) is
$\sum_{constantindex=1}^{settledlevel} \binom{settledlevel-1}{constantindex-1}/(2^{settledlevel-1} (constantindex+fixedcounter-1))$, and so
$startnumber = \frac{1}{2^{negativebound-1}} \sum_{constantindex=1}^{negativebound} \binom{negativebound-1}{constantindex-1}/constantindex$.
Now $\binom{negativebound-1}{constantindex-1}/constantindex = \binom{negativebound}{constantindex}/negativebound$; hence
\[
startnumber = \frac{1}{negativebound 2^{negativebound-1}} \sum_{constantindex=1}^{negativebound} \binom{negativebound}{constantindex}
= \frac{2^{negativebound}-1}{negativebound 2^{negativebound-1}} < 2/negativebound,
\]
as desired.

garbled_string.question:
Let  $lkjhgfds$  be a positive integer. Starting with the sequence
$1, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{3}, \dots, \frac{1}{lkjhgfds}$,
form a new sequence of  $lkjhgfds-1$  entries
$\frac{3}{4}, \frac{5}{12}, \dots, \frac{2lkjhgfds-1}{2lkjhgfds(lkjhgfds-1)}$
by taking the averages of
two consecutive entries in the first sequence. Repeat the
averaging of neighbors on the second sequence to obtain a third
sequence of  $lkjhgfds-2$  entries, and continue until the final sequence produced
consists of a single number  $azsxdcfv$.  Show that  $azsxdcfv < 2/lkjhgfds$.
garbled_string.solution:
It is easy to see by induction that the $hjgrksla$-th entry of the $mnbvcxzq$-th
sequence (where the original sequence is $mnbvcxzq=1$) is
$\sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{mnbvcxzq} \binom{mnbvcxzq-1}{qzxwvtnp-1}/(2^{mnbvcxzq-1} (qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla-1))$, and so
$azsxdcfv = \frac{1}{2^{lkjhgfds-1}} \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{lkjhgfds} \binom{lkjhgfds-1}{qzxwvtnp-1}/qzxwvtnp$.
Now $\binom{lkjhgfds-1}{qzxwvtnp-1}/qzxwvtnp = \binom{lkjhgfds}{qzxwvtnp}/lkjhgfds$; hence
\[
azsxdcfv = \frac{1}{lkjhgfds2^{lkjhgfds-1}} \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{lkjhgfds} \binom{lkjhgfds}{qzxwvtnp}
= \frac{2^{lkjhgfds}-1}{lkjhgfds 2^{lkjhgfds-1}} < 2/lkjhgfds,
\]
as desired.

kernel_variant.question:
Fix two integers $d\ge 1$ and $n\ge 2$.  Put  
\[
A_{0}(i_{1},\dots ,i_{d})=\frac{7}{\,i_{1}+\dots +i_{d}-d+1\,},
\qquad 1\le i_{1},\dots ,i_{d}\le n .
\]

For every integer $t$ with $0\le t\le n-2$ define  
\[
m_{t}:=n-t\quad\bigl(\text{so }m_{0}=n,\;m_{n-1}=1\bigr).
\]
The array $A_{t}$ has side length $m_{t}$.  
Construct the next array $A_{t+1}$ (side length $m_{t+1}=m_{t}-1$) by averaging, for every  
\[
1\le j_{1},\dots ,j_{d}\le m_{t+1},
\]
over the $2^{d}$ vertices of the axis-parallel unit hyper-cube  
\[
H_{t}(j_{1},\dots ,j_{d})
      :=\{(j_{1}+\varepsilon_{1},\dots ,j_{d}+\varepsilon_{d})
          \mid \varepsilon_{k}\in\{0,1\}\}.
\]
Precisely,
\[
A_{t+1}(j_{1},\dots ,j_{d})
         :=2^{-d}\!\!\sum_{\varepsilon\in\{0,1\}^{d}}
                A_{t}(j_{1}+\varepsilon_{1},\dots ,j_{d}+\varepsilon_{d}).
\]

After $n-1$ rounds exactly one entry is left; denote this number by $z_{n,d}$.

a)  Prove the closed formula  
\[
\boxed{\;
        z_{n,d}= \frac{14\bigl(1-2^{-d(n-1)-1}\bigr)}
                       {\,d(n-1)+1\,}
      \;}. \tag{$\star$}
\]

b)  Deduce the sharp universal bound
\[
\boxed{\;z_{n,d}\le \dfrac{14}{\,d(n-1)+1\,}\;},
\]
showing at the same time that the inequality is always strict but that
the ratio of the two sides tends to $1$ as $d(n-1)\to\infty$.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout put $m_{t}=n-t$.

Step 0.  One-dimensional weights after an arbitrary number of rounds  
For a vector $x=(x_{1},\dots ,x_{m})$ define the linear operator
\[
(T_{m}x)_{j}:=\frac{x_{j}+x_{j+1}}{2},\qquad 1\le j\le m-1 .
\]
A direct induction on $t$ shows that after $t$ consecutive neighbour-averagings
the coefficient of the original entry $x_{i}$ in the $j$-th component of the
shortened vector is
\[
2^{-t}\binom{t}{\,i-j\,},\qquad
1\le j\le m-t,\;j\le i\le j+t .
\]
In particular, after the full $m-1$ rounds that leave only a single number
the weight of $x_{i}$ equals
\[
2^{-(m-1)}\binom{m-1}{\,i-1\,}. \tag{1D}
\]

Step 1.  Averaging operators in $d$ dimensions  
Let $\mathcal T_{m}$ denote the $d$-dimensional averaging operator that sends
an $m^{d}$ array $A$ to the $(m-1)^{d}$ array obtained by one cube-averaging:
\[
(\mathcal T_{m}A)(j_{1},\dots ,j_{d})
     =2^{-d}\!\!\sum_{\varepsilon\in\{0,1\}^{d}}
            A(j_{1}+\varepsilon_{1},\dots ,j_{d}+\varepsilon_{d}).
\]
For every coordinate $\nu$ put
\[
T_{m}^{(\nu)}:=\underbrace{I\otimes\dots\otimes I}_{\nu-1}
               \otimes\,T_{m}\,\otimes
               \underbrace{I\otimes\dots\otimes I}_{d-\nu},
\]
where $I$ is the identity on $\mathbb R^{m}$.
Because averaging over a cube can be performed by successively averaging
along each axis, one has the factorisation
\[
\mathcal T_{m}=T_{m}^{(1)}\circ\dots\circ T_{m}^{(d)}
              =T_{m}^{\otimes d}. \tag{1}
\]

Step 2.  Composition of the successive rounds  
Starting from $A_{0}$ of side length $n=m_{0}$ we successively apply
$\mathcal T_{m_{0}},\mathcal T_{m_{1}},\dots ,\mathcal T_{m_{n-2}}$.
By equation (1) this equals the composition
\[
A_{n-1}
  = \bigl(T_{m_{n-1}}^{\otimes d}\bigr)\circ
    \bigl(T_{m_{n-2}}^{\otimes d}\bigr)\circ\dots\circ
    \bigl(T_{m_{0}}^{\otimes d}\bigr)(A_{0}). \tag{2}
\]
Each factor $T_{m}^{\otimes d}$ acts only on arrays whose side length is
exactly $m$, so all terms in the composition are well defined.

Step 3.  The weight of an initial entry  
Fix an initial index $(i_{1},\dots ,i_{d})$ with $1\le i_{\nu}\le n$.
Because the operators acting along different axes commute, the overall
weight with which this entry contributes to the final single number is the
product of the one-dimensional weights from (1D):
\[
w(i_{1},\dots ,i_{d})
   =\prod_{\nu=1}^{d}2^{-(n-1)}\binom{n-1}{\,i_{\nu}-1\,}
   =2^{-d(n-1)}
     \prod_{\nu=1}^{d}\binom{n-1}{\,i_{\nu}-1\,}. \tag{3}
\]
The family $\{w(i_{1},\dots ,i_{d})\}$ forms a probability distribution,
since each one-dimensional weight sequence is stochastic.

Step 4.  Probabilistic reformulation  
Introduce independent random variables
$X_{1},\dots ,X_{d}$ with distribution
\[
\mathbb P\!\bigl[X_{\nu}=i\bigr]
     =2^{-(n-1)}\binom{n-1}{\,i-1\,},\qquad i=1,\dots ,n .
\]
With $S:=X_{1}+\dots +X_{d}$ the representation (3) gives
\[
z_{n,d}=7\,\mathbb E\!\bigl[S-d+1\bigr]^{-1}. \tag{4}
\]

Step 5.  Converting the reciprocal into an integral  
For any integer $m\ge 1$,
\[
\frac{1}{m}=\int_{0}^{1}t^{\,m-1}\,dt .
\]
Taking $m=S-d+1$ in (4) yields
\[
z_{n,d}=7\int_{0}^{1}\mathbb E\!\bigl[t^{\,S-d}\bigr]\,dt
       =7\int_{0}^{1}\prod_{\nu=1}^{d}\mathbb E\!\bigl[t^{\,X_{\nu}-1}\bigr]\,dt. \tag{5}
\]

Step 6.  Evaluating the inner expectation  
For every $\nu$,
\[
\mathbb E\!\bigl[t^{\,X_{\nu}-1}\bigr]
   =2^{-(n-1)}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\binom{n-1}{\,i-1\,}t^{\,i-1}
   =2^{-(n-1)}(1+t)^{\,n-1}. \tag{6}
\]
Insert (6) into (5) and denote $M:=d(n-1)$:
\[
z_{n,d}=7\cdot 2^{-M}\int_{0}^{1}(1+t)^{M}\,dt. \tag{7}
\]

Step 7.  A Beta-type integral  
\[
\int_{0}^{1}(1+t)^{M}\,dt
     =\Bigl[\frac{(1+t)^{M+1}}{M+1}\Bigr]_{0}^{1}
     =\frac{2^{M+1}-1}{M+1}. \tag{8}
\]
Combining (7) and (8) gives
\[
z_{n,d}
   =7\cdot 2^{-M}\cdot\frac{2^{M+1}-1}{M+1}
   =\frac{14\bigl(1-2^{-(M+1)}\bigr)}{M+1}. \tag{9}
\]
Remembering $M=d(n-1)$ establishes the closed formula $(\star)$.

Step 8.  The uniform bound  
Because $1-2^{-(M+1)}<1$ for every finite $M$,
\[
z_{n,d}
   =\frac{14\bigl(1-2^{-(M+1)}\bigr)}{M+1}
   <\frac{14}{M+1}
   =\frac{14}{\,d(n-1)+1\,}. \tag{10}
\]
Replacing the strict inequality by $\le$ does not affect truth, hence
part (b) follows.  Equality would force $2^{-(M+1)}=0$, impossible for
finite $M$; thus the inequality is always strict.
Since $1-2^{-(M+1)}\to 1$ as $M\to\infty$, the ratio of the two sides
tends to $1$, proving optimality.

Remark.  The argument never used $d\ge 2$; for $d=1$ it reduces to the
classical neighbour-averaging problem with the initial harmonic sequence.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension.  
 The original single–index process is promoted to an averaging
procedure on a d-dimensional lattice; the relevant combinatorics now
involves d independent binomial distributions and a d-fold convolution.

2. Sophisticated tools.  
 To keep the problem tractable the solution introduces a probabilistic
model, integral transforms, the Beta function and properties of the
gamma function—none of which appear in the original exercise.

3. Additional layers of reasoning.  
 Deriving the common weight (1) already requires an
induction-on-dimension argument; converting the discrete expectation
to the integral (7) and evaluating it with a non-trivial substitution
is another conceptual leap.

4. Sharper estimates.  
 After obtaining an exact integral expression the solver still needs a
careful chain of inequalities—(9) through (13)—to finish the bound
requested.  Balancing factorials against powers demands good mastery
of asymptotic or combinatorial bounds.

5. Interacting concepts.  
 Combinatorics (multinomial weights), analysis (integral
representations and Beta functions) and probability (interpretation of
the weights as a distribution, expectation manipulation) all interact
essentially; no single elementary trick suffices.

Because of these extra dimensions—literally and figuratively—the
enhanced variant is substantially more challenging than both the
original problem and the one–dimensional kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
